metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rename the epic title to remove 'serial->batching' (e.g., 'Parallel-agent auto-lander for main — Phase 1: serial lander') so the title matches the committed scope.
findings.finding: The epic title 'serial->batching' signals a serial-to-batching progression, but the body explicitly rejects batching as 'evaluated and rejected' and defers the throughput follow-on to a separate idea. An executor encountering the title cold would expect batching to be a deliverable.
findings.location: Epic title: 'Parallel-agent auto-lander for main — serial->batching (project infrastructure)'
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Parallel-agent auto-lander for main — Phase 1: serial lander (project infrastructure)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default queue ordering for competing Autosubmit stacks (e.g., 'FIFO by the timestamp the Autosubmit label was cast; ties broken by lowest change number') so the executor need not invent this policy.
findings.finding: When multiple stacks simultaneously hold `Autosubmit +1`, the plan does not state the queue ordering the bot uses to select which stack to attempt first. 'Front Autosubmit+submittable change/chain' is used but 'front' is undefined across competing stacks.
findings.location: AC Phase 1, bullet 2: 'for the front Autosubmit+submittable change/chain it rebases to the current tip'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the serial lander AC: 'When multiple stacks hold Autosubmit, the bot processes them FIFO by label-cast timestamp (ties: lowest Gerrit change number first).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign creation of the pre-cutover `GET /projects/rebar/access` snapshot to a specific story (e.g., S6 or the label-config story) and name the file/location where it is committed (e.g., `infra/gerrit/access-snapshot-pre-autolander.json`) so the diff is executable in-session.
findings.finding: The security-invariants AC requires 'a GET /projects/rebar/access diff vs the pre-cutover snapshot' as the in-session proof that the two-vote gate is unweakened, but the plan does not state where the pre-cutover snapshot lives, who creates it, or which story creates it. If no snapshot exists when the invariants section is written, the verification step is unexecutable.
findings.location: AC Phase 1, last bullet: 'Security-invariants artifact… a GET /projects/rebar/access diff vs the pre-cutover snapshot'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Autosubmit label AC (or S6): 'Before pushing the label config, capture `GET /projects/rebar/access` and commit the response as `infra/gerrit/access-snapshot-pre-autolander.json`; the ADR-0042 invariants section diffs the post-cutover response against this file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default await-timeout for Verified (e.g., 'if no Verified vote arrives within 2× the typical CI wall-clock (configurable, default 60 min), remove Autosubmit and record a ci_failed outcome') so the executor need not invent this policy.
findings.finding: The plan says the lander 'awaits a fresh Verified +1' but does not state a timeout or the handling path when Verified never arrives (e.g., GHA outage, job lost). Without a stated default the executor must invent a timeout/no-timeout policy.
findings.location: AC Phase 1, bullet 2: 'awaits a fresh Verified +1'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the serial lander AC: 'If no Verified vote is received within a configurable timeout (default: 60 min), the lander removes Autosubmit and records a ci_failed outcome, releasing wipChain for the next stack.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'bot is the single source of truth' claim with the Outcome provenance split: either (a) clarify that 'single source of truth' means the *land command* is the single consumer API and the underlying authority differs per outcome (bot state for needs_rebase/ci_failed, Gerrit native status for merged/review_failed), or (b) have the bot record all four outcomes in its state so the claim is literally true — and update the AC to reflect whichever model is chosen.
findings.finding: The Context section asserts 'the bot is the single source of truth for the outcome,' but immediately qualifies that `merged` and `review_failed` outcomes 'are derived from native Gerrit status / an LLM-Review -1 by the land command (S4)' — i.e., derived by the CLI, not recorded by the bot. The AC Phase 1 repeats 'The bot is the single source of truth for the outcome' without acknowledging this split. An implementer reading the AC alone would believe all four outcomes flow through the bot's state, contradicting the Outcome provenance note in Context.
findings.location: Context § 'Agent contract' / AC Phase 1 'Agent landing contract' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence clarification to the AC bullet: 'The land command is the single agent-facing source of truth; internally, needs_rebase and ci_failed are read from bot state (S3), while merged and review_failed are derived directly from Gerrit primitives by the land command (S4).' Remove or qualify the unqualified 'bot is the single source of truth' phrasing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC criterion specifying the outcome-record schema (at minimum: required fields — e.g. change_id, stack_root, outcome enum value, timestamp, optional detail string — and where it is stored in S3 state) so S3 and S4 have a shared contract.
findings.finding: The Greenfield note lists 'the outcome-record schema' as a new artifact created by this epic, but no AC criterion covers its structure, required fields, or contract. The AC only references 'its own state (S3)' in passing; the schema definition itself is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Greenfield note / AC Phase 1 (no matching criterion)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback / back-out plan for the `refs/meta/config` changes (label + ACL) and the auto-lander service — e.g., a pre-authored revert commit for `project.config` and a `docker compose down` / feature-flag procedure to restore manual-only landing.
findings.finding: The plan makes a semi-irreversible Gerrit `refs/meta/config` change (new label + ACL grant) and deploys a new service that mediates landing, but specifies no explicit rollback or back-out plan if the auto-lander proves buggy or the label needs removal post-deployment.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / Phase 1 — serial auto-lander, first two bullet points; Context ('Gate substrate stays')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection to the AC (or ADR-0042) describing: (1) a pre-authored `refs/meta/config` revert commit that removes the `Autosubmit` label and ACL grant; (2) the compose command to stop the service; (3) confirmation that manual submit continues to work with the service stopped (already implied by 'coexists with manual submit' but should be explicit).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Constrain the `CLAUDE.md`/`CONTRIBUTING.md` doc update to land in the same story (or as a hard dependency of) the `land` command delivery, AND add a one-time sweep of other repo docs (README, onboarding, wiki) for stale manual-rebase-retry references.
findings.finding: The plan requires rewriting `CLAUDE.md` + `CONTRIBUTING.md` to the new landing contract but does not specify sequencing relative to service/command availability, risking a window where agents read docs that reference a `land` command that doesn't yet exist. It also does not call for a sweep of any other docs that reference the old manual rebase-retry workflow.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / Phase 1 — 'Docs: rewrite this repo's landing section (CLAUDE.md + CONTRIBUTING.md)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the story that delivers the `land` command (the 'lead story'), add the CLAUDE.md/CONTRIBUTING.md update as a same-PR requirement, not a separate deliverable. Add a single AC bullet: 'No other file in the repo still instructs manual rebase-retry as the primary landing path (grep check).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a probe/RED fixture (or reference an existing one) that exercises `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` with `on_behalf_of_uploader` against this project's Gerrit instance and asserts that author, DCO sign-off, and `rebar-ticket` trailers are present in the resulting patchset's commit message before committing to the full serial-lander build-out.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `on_behalf_of_uploader` on the Gerrit rebase API will preserve author identity, DCO sign-off, and `rebar-ticket` trailers through a server-side rebase, but includes no spike, RED fixture, or probe that verifies this claim against this project's actual Gerrit instance and server-side rebase strategy. The API parameter is an advertised contract, but whether server-side rebase preserves commit-message trailers (rather than re-generating a clean commit message) is a project-specific integration point whose correctness cannot be established by reasoning alone.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC: 'Serial auto-lander service' bullet — '`on_behalf_of_uploader` to preserve author/DCO/`rebar-ticket` trailers'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-story spike (or a single integration test in the S1/S2 story) that: (1) creates a test change with a known trailer set, (2) calls the rebase API with `on_behalf_of_uploader`, and (3) asserts trailer presence via `GET /changes/{id}/revisions/current/commit`. Gate the serial-lander implementation stories on this probe's green result.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (in ADR-0042 or the outcome-record schema definition story) a versioning strategy for the S3 outcome-record schema — e.g., a `schema_version` field and a forward-compatibility rule — so that Phase-2 additions and the eventual provider-seam refactor do not silently corrupt or misread existing records.
findings.finding: The outcome-record schema persisted to S3 (fields for `merged | needs_rebase | ci_failed | review_failed` and their provenance metadata) is introduced as a new data contract in this epic, but the plan contains no mention of schema versioning or a changeability strategy. When Phase 2 (bounded speculation, idea `c3a9-5f4c-bdc6-4e27`) or the provider-seam idea (`1701-8bc1-e3b7-4984`) arrives, this schema will need to evolve; without a versioning plan, callers of the land-status command may break silently on schema drift.
findings.location: Context / AC: 'Outcome provenance: the bot *records* `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` in its own state (S3)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `schema_version: 1` field to the outcome-record and document a policy (e.g., 'unknown fields are ignored; missing version treated as v1') in ADR-0042's data-contract section. The land-status command should log a warning on unknown schema_version rather than crashing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle the epic to reflect its actual committed scope (serial-only auto-lander), e.g. 'Parallel-agent auto-lander for main — serial lander (Phase 1, project infrastructure)', and clarify that 'serial->batching' was the evaluated progression, not the deliverable.
findings.finding: The epic title promises 'serial->batching' but the plan body commits only to the serial phase and explicitly rejects cross-stack batching on this infra (archiving stories S7-S9, deferring the throughput follow-on to idea c3a9-5f4c-bdc6-4e27). A reader or agent picking up this epic from its title would expect a batching deliverable that does not exist here.
findings.location: Epic title / Phase 2 section / Out of scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Parallel-agent auto-lander for main — serial lander (project infrastructure)' and add a one-liner in the title section: 'Phase 2 batching was evaluated and deferred; see idea c3a9-5f4c-bdc6-4e27.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider merging S2 and S3 into a single story ('lander service: happy path + failure handling') so the 'never partial-land' invariant is verifiable end-to-end within one deliverable, since neither half is independently deployable or safe without the other.
findings.finding: S2 (happy-path core loop: rebase:chain → await Verified → ancestor-atomic submit) and S3 (failure handling: 409 / CI-fail / never-partial-land) are tightly coupled halves of the same lander-service concern. S2 alone is not safely deployable: a service that handles only the success path and has undefined behavior on a 409 conflict or CI failure would violate the 'never partial-land' invariant the moment any real failure occurs. Neither story delivers independent value without the other, which fails the VALUE-PRESERVATION test for a valid story split.
findings.location: Children S2 (perovskite-frilly-grebe) and S3 (darksome-starry-sable)
findings.suggested_fix: Merge S2 (perovskite-frilly-grebe) and S3 (darksome-starry-sable) into one story whose AC covers both the success path and all failure modes (409, CI-fail, partial-land guard). The combined story remains a single coherent concern — the lander service loop — and is small enough to execute in one session given the service is a simple state machine over a few Gerrit API calls.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified failure branch for the submit POST returning a non-FFO conflict error (e.g., 'on Gerrit 409/non-FFO: clear Autosubmit, record needs_rebase outcome, hand stack back') so the re-verify check-then-act has a complete error path rather than an implicit crash or hang.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 're-verify is:submittable on the exact tested SHA immediately before submit' as the security backstop against a stale tree landing, but the submit step is a check-then-act over a live distributed system: GET is:submittable is followed by POST submit with no atomicity guarantee. In the concurrent parallel-agent scenario the plan explicitly solves, another stack can be submitted between those two calls, advancing main and making the FFO submit fail. The plan does not specify what the lander does on this submit-failure path: retry (looping back into the poll-and-rebase cycle), hand back as needs_rebase, or log and stop. The security invariant is preserved (Gerrit's FFO rejects the submit), but the lander's behavior after the rejection is an unspecified edge case that implementers will have to invent.
findings.location: AC Phase 1 — Security-invariants artifact bullet: 're-verify is:submittable on the exact tested SHA immediately before submit'; also the 'ancestor-atomic submits the exact tested SHA' description in the serial auto-lander AC bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the lander service AC or ADR-0042, add: 'If the ancestor-atomic submit returns a non-FFO conflict (Gerrit 409 or similar), the lander treats this identically to a rebase-conflict: clear Autosubmit, record needs_rebase, and hand the stack back to its owning agent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the wipChain serializes the ENTIRE pipeline per stack (rebase → await Verified → submit as one unit), not just the rebase step, so implementers understand that the next stack is not picked up until the current stack fully completes or is handed back.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a single wipChain (serialized, one instance) as the concurrency control that prevents two stacks racing each other. However, the plan does not specify the scope of the wipChain lock — whether it covers only the rebase step, the entire rebase→await-Verified→submit pipeline, or just the submit step. If the lock is held only during rebase (a natural implementation boundary) and released while waiting for Verified, two stacks could be in the await-Verified phase simultaneously; the first to become submittable would race the second for the submit, potentially violating the 'never partial-land' invariant for the second stack if the lander has already begun its submit sequence.
findings.location: AC Phase 1 — serial auto-lander service bullet: 'Single wipChain (serialized, one instance)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the lander service AC: 'The wipChain processes one chain end-to-end (rebase through submit) before picking up the next Autosubmit candidate; CI wait does not release the chain to a concurrent stack.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (via a Gerrit 3.14.1 API test or documentation citation) that `on_behalf_of_uploader` on `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` and `/rebase:chain` preserves all commit trailers (`Signed-off-by`, `rebar-ticket`, `Change-Id`) through the server-side rebase; document the finding in ADR-0042's Security Invariants section.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `on_behalf_of_uploader` on the Gerrit rebase API (`POST /changes/{id}/rebase`, `/rebase:chain`) will 'preserve author/DCO/rebar-ticket trailers' through a server-side rebase, but does not cite evidence that Gerrit 3.14.1's server-side rebase implementation actually carries commit trailers (Signed-off-by, rebar-ticket) intact. Server-side rebase rewrites commits; trailer preservation depends on undocumented internal behavior, and this assertion is load-bearing for DCO compliance and ticket traceability.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — Serial auto-lander service bullet: 'on_behalf_of_uploader to preserve author/DCO/rebar-ticket trailers'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-off manual test against the Gerrit 3.14.1 instance: rebase a change with a known DCO trailer using on_behalf_of_uploader and inspect the resulting commit object's message via `GET /changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/commit`. Record the outcome in ADR-0042. If trailers are not preserved, the autolander must strip-and-reapply them client-side before the rebase, or use a client-side git rebase instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an 'Alternatives considered for the autolander service' note (in the Context or a dedicated ADR-0042 section) that explicitly names and disposes of existing Gerrit submit-queue plugins or LF releng tooling, mirroring the ADR-0022 prior-art survey discipline used for the CI bridge.
findings.finding: The plan builds a bespoke autolander service but does not document whether any existing Gerrit plugin or LF project tooling (e.g. a Gerrit submit-queue plugin, or tooling from the same Linux Foundation ecosystem that produced gerrit-to-platform) was evaluated as a candidate to adopt wholesale, as required by T1(a). The plan's strong prior-art discipline for the CI bridge (ADR-0022: surveyed ONAP/O-RAN-SC/Go/Chromium before adopting g2p) is absent for the autolander service architecture.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — Serial auto-lander service bullet; Context section 'Design settled in the brainstorm'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and IaC the autolander's outcome-record storage: if S3, add an `aws_s3_bucket` resource with `prevent_destroy`, a scoped `s3:PutObject/GetObject/ListBucket` inline policy on the instance role (or switch to the EBS-backed state dir already used by the review-bot and remove the S3 reference).
findings.finding: The plan's Context section states the bot 'records needs_rebase/ci_failed in its own state (S3)' but provides no IaC for an application-purpose S3 bucket, no IAM grant to the instance role for s3:PutObject/GetObject, and no lifecycle or prevent_destroy on that bucket. The existing Terraform has no application-purpose S3 bucket — only the Terraform state backend bucket — and there is no mention of whether this is a new bucket or an existing one, what its access controls are, or which story owns its IaC.
findings.location: Context section — 'Outcome provenance: the bot records needs_rebase/ci_failed in its own state (S3)'
findings.suggested_fix: Consider storing outcome records in the EBS-backed state dir (a new external volume like `gerrit_autolander` created by compose-up.sh), consistent with the review-bot pattern, which eliminates the new S3 dependency and all associated IAM/lifecycle gaps. If S3 is preferred, add an `aws_s3_bucket.autolander_outcomes` resource in `infra/terraform/` with `prevent_destroy = true`, server-side encryption, and a scoped `s3:PutObject/GetObject/DeleteObject/ListBucket` inline policy on `aws_iam_role.gerrit_instance`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an autolander-specific CloudWatch alarm (e.g., `rebar-autolander-errors` in a new `monitoring_autolander.tf`) following the `monitoring_s4b.tf` / `monitoring_s5.tf` pattern: define a structured error marker the autolander logs on failure, have the host observability probe grep for it and publish a custom metric, and alarm on that metric.
findings.finding: The plan says the autolander 'logs to its own service logs (container stdout → existing CloudWatch monitoring)' but does not specify a new CloudWatch alarm for autolander-specific failure signals. The project's own established pattern (monitoring_s4b.tf for voter_errors, monitoring_s5.tf for replication_errors) requires an explicit alarm per new service failure mode. The autolander has distinct, actionable failure modes — a stuck wipChain, a crash loop, or repeated lander errors — that 'existing CloudWatch monitoring' (host-up/Gerrit-reachable alarms) will not detect.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Observability bullet: 'the bot logs to its own service logs (container stdout → existing CloudWatch monitoring)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State how the `refs/meta/config` push for the `Autosubmit` label is made idempotent (e.g., the push script checks current config before pushing, or the `setup-project.sh` pattern uses a force-push that always converges to the committed state) and how drift from the committed `infra/gerrit/project.config` is detected and alerted.
findings.finding: The plan adds the `Autosubmit` label to Gerrit's `refs/meta/config` via a git push (outside Terraform IaC), but does not address idempotency of that push or drift recovery if the label config is modified or deleted out-of-band. The existing `setup-project.sh` / `refs/meta/config` push pattern is `DETECT-ONLY` in autodeploy (per autodeploy.sh CONFIG_PATHS), meaning out-of-band changes to `project.config` are signalled but never auto-corrected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Phase 1, first bullet: '`Autosubmit` Gerrit label authored in `infra/gerrit/project.config` / pushed to `refs/meta/config`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike or reference a concrete probe (curl against the live Gerrit 3.14.1 instance) verifying the exact path and request body for both `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` (single) and the chain-rebase endpoint, including the 409-on-conflict response contract, before committing the full implementation.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the autolander will use `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` (single-change) and `/rebase:chain` (relation-chain) Gerrit REST endpoints, but neither endpoint appears anywhere in the existing codebase (gerrit_client.py, infra/gerrit/*.sh, e2e scripts). These are new endpoint surfaces on an already-used vendor, requiring their own empirical verification against Gerrit 3.14.1.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC: 'Serial auto-lander service' bullet — 'POST /changes/{id}/rebase / /rebase:chain'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the auth precondition for `on_behalf_of_uploader` (which Gerrit ACL the autolander bot identity needs, e.g. 'Run As' or Administrator), add that grant to `infra/gerrit/project.config` or `setup-ci-service-account.sh`, and add a probe or RED fixture that verifies trailer preservation after a server-side rebase on this Gerrit 3.14.1 instance.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `on_behalf_of_uploader` as a body parameter on the rebase POST to preserve author identity, DCO sign-off, and `rebar-ticket` trailers through a server-side rebase. This parameter is not used anywhere in the existing codebase, and the plan neither names the bot identity that must hold the required Gerrit ACL to act on behalf of another user, nor specifies what that ACL is.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC: 'Serial auto-lander service' bullet — '`on_behalf_of_uploader` to preserve author/DCO/`rebar-ticket` trailers'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `runAs = group Service Users` under `[access "refs/*"]` in `infra/gerrit/project.config` (or scope to refs/heads/main), and add a step in the autolander spike that does a live `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` with `on_behalf_of_uploader=true` and inspects the resulting patchset commit message for the DCO and rebar-ticket trailers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and verify the exact mechanism for atomic chain submit on Gerrit 3.14.1 (topic-based via `submitWholeTopic`, relation-chain cascade, or both), and add a live probe or e2e scenario that exercises multi-change chain submit under FFO to confirm no partial-land occurs.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'ancestor-atomic submit' for a relation chain via the existing `POST /changes/{id}/submit` endpoint, but the atomic chain-submit behavior on Gerrit 3.14.1 under FFO depends on whether the changes share a topic and `submitWholeTopic = true`, or whether the chain's dependency graph drives implicit cascading. The plan does not state which mechanism it relies on, and chain-atomic submit is not exercised anywhere in the existing e2e scripts.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC: 'Serial auto-lander service' bullet — 'ancestor-atomic submits the exact tested SHA'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that agent Gerrit identities are (or will be added as) members of the Contributors group, and add a note in `setup-project.sh` about keeping agent identities in Contributors; also clarify whether Gerrit's standard label vote ACL enforces 'vote only on your own change' or whether the plan assumes Contributors membership is the sole scope control.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `Autosubmit` Gerrit label with `label-Autosubmit = 0..+1 group Contributors` — granting vote permission to a non-Service-User, non-Administrator group. All existing label grants (`LLM-Review`, `Verified`) are restricted to `Service Users` and `Administrators` only. This is the first time a non-bot group is granted a label vote in this project, and the plan does not verify whether Gerrit 3.14.1 correctly enforces the `requester-votable` (self-vote only) constraint or whether the Contributors group definition needs updating before the grant works.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC: '`Autosubmit` Gerrit label' bullet — 'requester-votable ACL (vote granted to group `Contributors`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the autolander bot's Gerrit identity and credential lifecycle in ADR-0042's Security invariants section: whether it uses a dedicated service account or the existing review-bot account, what Gerrit group membership (and therefore `Submit` ACL coverage) it requires, and whether a new `/rebar/prod/autolander-*` SSM SecureString slot is provisioned — consistent with the established SSM → .env → container pattern.
findings.finding: The autolander bot's Gerrit identity, required ACL grants, and credential lifecycle are undeclared. The bot must call POST /changes/{id}/rebase, remove the Autosubmit label, and submit changes — the last of which requires `submit` permission (currently restricted to `Contributors` + `Administrators` by the exclusive grant in project.config). The plan does not declare: (a) whether the autolander uses the existing review-bot identity, a new dedicated service account, or another identity; (b) what Gerrit group membership (and therefore submit permission) the autolander's account needs; (c) whether a new SSM SecureString slot is provisioned for the autolander's HTTP password or SSH key. This is an undeclared access posture on the boundary-crossing Gerrit API submit path.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — 'Serial auto-lander service' bullet; 'Agent landing contract' bullet; implicit deployment pattern 'deploys like the review-bot'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated Gerrit service account (e.g. `autolander-bot`) as a `Service User` AND add it to the `Contributors` group (or grant it an explicit `submit` in project.config under a `Service Users` group rule). Provision a new SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` and add it to the `rebar_secret_params` list in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`. Add a `label-Autosubmit = -1..+1 group Service Users` ACL entry in project.config alongside the `Contributors` grant (so the bot can remove the label). Document all of this in ADR-0042's Security invariants section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the outcome-record schema (field names, types, schema version, serialization format) co-located with or directly referenced from the AC bullet that introduces it, so all stories implementing the `land-status` command share an identical contract.
findings.finding: The 'outcome-record schema' stored in the bot's own state (S3) is referenced as a new artifact but its field vocabulary is never defined: no field names, types, schema version, or serialization format appear anywhere in the plan. An implementer of `land-status` must invent the schema, creating a contractual gap between the command and the bot's state.
findings.location: Context section: 'Outcome provenance: the bot *records* `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` in its own state (S3)'; AC: 'outcome-record schema' listed as a new artifact
findings.suggested_fix: Add an inline schema stub (e.g. `{stack_id: str, change_ids: list[str], outcome: Literal['needs_rebase','ci_failed'], recorded_at: ISO8601, conflict_change_id?: str}`) to the AC bullet or a linked design note, and cross-reference it from both the bot story and the land-command story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decision-rule table or ordered lookup sequence (e.g. Gerrit status → LLM-Review vote → S3 record) to the 'Agent landing contract' AC bullet so the `land-status` command's resolution logic is fully specified in one place.
findings.finding: The two-path outcome-provenance rule — `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` are *recorded* (S3) while `merged`/`review_failed` are *derived* at query time — is stated only in the Context section and is not co-located with the AC bullet defining the `land`/`land-status` command's resolution logic. An implementer of the land command must synthesize this rule from two disconnected places, and the exact decision sequence (check S3 first? check Gerrit first? what wins on conflict?) is never specified.
findings.location: Context: 'Outcome provenance' paragraph; AC Phase 1: 'Agent landing contract' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `review_failed` detection rule for stacks: define exactly which patchsets and which changes in the chain are checked for `LLM-Review=-1`, and whether the check is against the current patchset only.
findings.finding: The `review_failed` outcome is described as 'derived from … an `LLM-Review -1`' but no rule specifies how this is determined for a stack (relation chain): does any change in the chain with LLM-Review=-1 trigger `review_failed`, or only the front change, or only changes the bot just rebased? The detection rule is absent.
findings.location: Context: 'Outcome provenance' paragraph; AC Phase 1: 'Agent landing contract' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC bullet: '`review_failed` = any change in the stack's current patchset carries LLM-Review=-1 at the moment of the re-verify check immediately before submit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a crash/restart fallback rule to the failure-handling AC bullet: specify what the bot writes to S3 on unclean shutdown (or how it detects a stale wipChain on startup), what `land-status` returns to the agent in this state, and whether recovery is automatic or requires agent intervention.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a `wipChain` (serialized, one instance) but provides no fallback for bot crash or restart while a chain is mid-rebase or mid-submit: what state is left in S3, what does `land-status` return to the agent, and does the bot auto-resume or require manual intervention? This incomplete-sub-step fallback is unspecified.
findings.location: AC Phase 1: 'Serial auto-lander service' bullet ('Single `wipChain` (serialized, one instance)'); AC: 'Failure handling' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete exit-code-to-outcome mapping table (e.g. 0=merged, 2=needs_rebase, 3=ci_failed, 4=review_failed) to the 'Agent landing contract' AC bullet so all stories and the docs update share an identical, non-guessed contract.
findings.finding: The plan promises 'distinct exit code' per outcome but never enumerates the exit-code-to-outcome mapping. Agents branch on exit codes; without the mapping table the contract is unimplementable without guessing.
findings.location: AC Phase 1: 'Agent landing contract' bullet: 'returns ONE typed terminal outcome … + distinct exit code'
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt the repo's existing exit-code convention (see `docs/exit-codes.md`) and extend it with 4 new codes for the lander outcomes, referenced from the AC bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the ci_failed persistence conflict: either update the parent's 'Outcome provenance' to match S3's live-derivation design (and confirm S4 AC5 is correct as written), or update S3 to persist ci_failed markers and align S4's fallback-precedence rule accordingly.
findings.finding: Parent 'Outcome provenance' states 'the bot *records* `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` in its own state (S3)' — explicitly naming both. S3's design directly contradicts this: S3 states 'persist ONLY `needs_rebase` markers' and '`ci_failed`… [is] NOT persisted — derived LIVE from Gerrit at read time.' S4's fallback-precedence rule (AC5) aligns with S3's live-derivation model, not the parent's recorded-ci_failed model. The parent's design description and child S3's authoritative design are in direct conflict on whether ci_failed is a persisted bot record or a live Gerrit derivation.
findings.location: child 0525-6d71-8969-4512 (S3) — 'Outcome record' section + 'Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache' AC; parent plan — 'Outcome provenance' paragraph under Agent contract
findings.suggested_fix: Align the parent 'Outcome provenance' text to S3's authoritative design: change 'the bot records `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed`' to 'the bot records ONLY `needs_rebase`'; `ci_failed` is derived live from a `Verified -1` vote on the current patchset. S4 AC5 is already correct as written.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify hand-back ownership: either move the N=5 TOCTOU hand-back AC entirely into S3 (and make S2's AC4 reference S3 as the provider), or accept that S2 implements the shared hand-back helper and S3 reuses it (reversing the dependency so S3's 'depends on S2' is correct but S3 does NOT re-implement the hand-back).
findings.finding: S2 AC4 (N=5 TOCTOU exhaustion) fully describes implementing the hand-back steps — 'the bot removes the `Autosubmit` label from every member… posts a Gerrit comment naming the reason… and writes S3's `needs_rebase` marker' — as S2's own deliverable. Yet S2's scope statement says 'The N=5-exhaustion park *reuses S3's `needs_rebase` hand-back mechanism*,' implying S3 owns the mechanism. S3's scope states it 'adds the failure/edge branches to S2's loop,' and S3 depends on S2 (not the reverse). This creates a circular ownership: S2 needs S3's hand-back code to exist to satisfy S2 AC4, but S3 cannot be built until S2's loop exists. Neither story cleanly owns a stub/interface that resolves the dependency.
findings.location: child 0ee8-08de-bf0f-45ec (S2) — AC4 'Hand-back mechanism' block + Scope paragraph 'N=5-exhaustion park'; child 0525-6d71-8969-4512 (S3) — Scope + Dependencies
findings.suggested_fix: Move the N=5 TOCTOU hand-back steps out of S2 AC4 into S3, and replace the S2 AC4 text with: 'On N=5 exhaustion, invoke the shared hand-back function (delivered by S3) — which removes Autosubmit, posts a comment, and writes the needs_rebase marker.' This keeps S3 as the single owner of all hand-back logic and preserves the S3-depends-on-S2 direction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove all references to S9 from S3's Outcome record section; update the consumer list to 'S4 (land command)' only, and the stack_id shared-key list to 'S2/S3/S4'.
findings.finding: S3's Outcome record section references 'S9 (batch failure)' twice — once in the consumer list ('S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache') and once in the stack_id description ('it is the shared key across S2/S3/S4/S9'). S9 is an archived story: the parent plan explicitly states 'S7-S9 archived' as part of the cross-stack batching rejection. These references to a non-existent sibling create dead cross-references that will mislead implementers.
findings.location: child 0525-6d71-8969-4512 (S3) — 'Outcome record' section, `stack_id` definition paragraph and consumer list
findings.suggested_fix: In S3 Outcome record, replace 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache' with 'S4 (land command) consumes this cache', and replace 'shared key across S2/S3/S4/S9' with 'shared key across S2/S3/S4'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign the ApprovalInfo.date population verification to S1's AC4 (as part of the live cutover verification step): after applying the label config, issue GET /changes/?q=label:Autosubmit+1&o=DETAILED_LABELS against a test change carrying the label and confirm ApprovalInfo.date is non-null in the response.
findings.finding: S2 AC1 includes an unresolved precondition parenthetical: 'Precondition to verify against the live Gerrit 3.14.1: that `ApprovalInfo.date` is populated for the custom `Autosubmit` label; existing repo code reads only the integer `value`.' No child in the sibling roster has an AC that assigns ownership of this verification. If `ApprovalInfo.date` is not returned for project-authored labels by this Gerrit version, FIFO ordering silently falls back to undefined behaviour (the field is absent/null) — the lander could select candidates in the wrong order with no error.
findings.location: child 0ee8-08de-bf0f-45ec (S2) — AC1, parenthetical precondition; no sibling owns the verification
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S1 AC4: 'After cutover, verify `ApprovalInfo.date` is non-null in a `GET /changes/?q=label:Autosubmit+1&o=DETAILED_LABELS` response for a change bearing the label — confirming the FIFO ordering field S2 depends on is populated by this Gerrit 3.14.1 instance for project-authored labels.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to S6 AC3: 'The ADR-0042 Security invariants section must enumerate ALL FOUR invariants from the parent AC — including (3) re-verify `is:submittable` on the exact tested SHA immediately before submit (with a pointer to the S2 unit test asserting this guard) and (4) two-vote gate unweakened (with a GET /projects/rebar/access diff vs pre-cutover snapshot) — not only the label/ACL invariants covered by S1.'
findings.finding: The parent's Security-invariants AC requires the ADR-0042 Security invariants section to enumerate ALL FOUR named invariants with their concrete checks: (1) non-gating Autosubmit, (2) requester-votable Contributors ACL tightly scoped, (3) re-verify `is:submittable` on the exact tested SHA immediately before submit, (4) two-vote gate unweakened. S1 AC5 covers invariants 1–2 with concrete grep checks. S6 (the ADR author) has no AC that explicitly requires the section to enumerate invariants 3–4 with their concrete proofs — a test asserting the re-verify logic and a GET /projects/rebar/access diff. S2's ACs contain the re-verify logic but do not link it to the ADR-0042 security section.
findings.location: child b825-a233-97d4-45c2 (S6) — AC3 (ADR-0042 scope); parent plan — Phase 1 AC 'Security-invariants artifact'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend S6 AC3 with: 'The Security invariants section enumerates all four parent-named invariants; for invariant (3) cite the S2 unit-test name that asserts the re-confirm-submittable pre-submit check; for invariant (4) include or reference the GET /projects/rebar/access diff artifact from S1's cutover verification.'

findings.finding: S3 uses stack_id as the canonical key for the needs_rebase marker ('keyed by change_id + patchset_SHA' then 'stack_id upsert key' — the text uses both), and says 'stack_id = the Gerrit topic the auto-lander assigns to a stack at selection time.' However, S2's wipChain schema definition (AC5) lists {change_id, chain_member_ids[], tested_shas, verified_at, phase, re_drive_count} with no stack_id field. The entity that assigns stack_id (S2 at selection time) does not include it in its own schema.
findings.location: child 0525-6d71-8969-4512 (S3) — Outcome record section; child 0ee8-08de-bf0f-45ec (S2) — AC5 wipChain schema
findings.suggested_fix: In S2 AC5, extend the wipChain schema to: '{change_id, chain_member_ids[], stack_id, tested_shas{change_id→sha}, verified_at{change_id→ts}, phase, re_drive_count}' with the note: 'stack_id is assigned at selection time as the Gerrit topic (or tip change_id if no topic is set); this is the shared key S3 uses to key its needs_rebase marker.'

findings.finding: The plan says `review_failed` is 'derived from … an `LLM-Review -1` by the land command (S4)', but the existing codebase defines two structurally distinct LLM-Review -1 kinds: `[LLM-Review: BLOCK — finding]` (agent must fix code) and `[LLM-Review: BLOCK — coverage-gap (...)]` (infra veto — not the agent's fault, maintainer re-triggers). The plan gives no decision rule for which -1 kind maps to `review_failed` vs. a different outcome, leaving the agent with an ambiguous and potentially wrong remediation signal.
findings.location: Context § Outcome provenance ('review_failed … derived from … an LLM-Review -1'); AC § Agent landing contract
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: `review_failed` = LLM-Review -1 tagged `[LLM-Review: BLOCK — finding]`; a coverage-gap -1 (`[LLM-Review: BLOCK — coverage-gap (...)]`) is treated as a transient infra failure and either retried or surfaced as a distinct `infra_error` outcome so the agent does not attempt to fix code.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
